digest: Standard-depth research found strong primary-source support for a layered safe-operation baseline for AI coding agents in local dev and CI; all 16 audited claims verified, with remaining caveats around agent-specific IR, version anchoring, CI egress variance, and optional IDE-agent coverage.

finalReport.summary: Working conclusion: operate AI coding agents as constrained, auditable, least-privilege automation rather than trusted developers. For a small team, the most supportable baseline is: default to read-only or workspace-scoped local permissions; keep network disabled or allowlisted; keep CI tokens/secrets least-privilege and gated; treat dependency installs/build scripts as high-risk code execution; require human approval for secrets, deploys, dependency policy changes, workflow changes, and broad tool/network access; retain enough logs/session traces for review while assuming logs may contain sensitive data; and prepare an incident checklist for exposed secrets or unsafe agent changes. This conclusion is based mainly on verified primary vendor/security sources, but some product behavior is live-doc/main-branch scoped and agent-specific incident-response guidance remains synthesized from broader IR sources.

researchMetadata.taskType: technical security research and operational guidance
researchMetadata.expectedFinalShape: bounded parent-facing research handoff with recommendations, action plan, caveats, and compact claim verdict index
researchMetadata.sourcePolicy: Primary vendor docs, official package docs, and recognized security guidance were preferred; audit-claims verdicts are used as authoritative claim-level status; preserved unverified leads are labeled and not used as hard thresholds.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini/GitHub provider docs provide security guidance on sandboxing, permissions, firewalling, and approvals.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Supports provider comparison for local and cloud agents.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Codex has read-only/workspace-write/danger-full-access; Claude Code Bash sandbox has platform-specific read/write boundaries; Gemini CLI supports multiple sandbox backends.
Codex network off by default in workspace-write; Claude prompts/managed domains; Copilot firewall default with caveats; GitLab/GitHub runner network controls remain platform-specific detail.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Supports network isolation recommendations and caveats.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Use least privilege, avoid plaintext/CLI secret exposure, use OIDC short-lived tokens where possible, preserve fork/MR secret restrictions, mask/audit secrets, and rotate/delete after exposure.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Core guidance on secrets in CI/local agent workflows.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: OWASP and NIST support layered prompt-injection controls: segregate untrusted content, least privilege, filtering/validation, adversarial testing, and approval for high-risk actions.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Prevents overreliance on prompts and frames coding-agent threat model.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: npm lifecycle scripts and pip distribution installs can execute code; mitigations include npm ci, --ignore-scripts when scripts are unnecessary, pip --require-hashes, --only-binary :all:, and controlled indexes/wheelhouses.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Supports high-risk classification of package-manager actions.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Claude prompts for edits/commands; OWASP recommends human approval for high-risk actions; GitHub environments gate secrets/deploys; GitLab fork MR parent-context runs require review/warnings.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Justifies human gates for secrets, deploys, dependencies, workflow changes, and broad tool permissions.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Copilot cloud agent provides session logs/commit links; GitHub Actions/audit logs and Claude cloud/local transcript settings provide traceability and retention/privacy tradeoffs.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Supports audit baseline and cautions about log confidentiality.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Direct GitHub step: delete exposed workflow logs and rotate secrets; NIST/CISA provide general IR phases to adapt for agent incidents.
factSlotCoverage.evidenceStatus: verified for GitHub secret-exposure step; synthesized for agent-specific IR mapping
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: IR checklist should distinguish primary facts from adapted procedure.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Low-cost controls include read-only CI tokens, per-job permissions, required reviewers for secrets/deploys, CODEOWNERS for workflow changes, audited logs, frozen installs, and version-controlled agent settings.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Enables prioritized recommendations suitable for small teams.

factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Vendor and security guidance consistently says sandboxing/firewalls/prompts reduce but do not eliminate risk; limits include firewall bypass/coverage, broad reads, DNS rebinding, and no foolproof prompt-injection prevention.
Evidence includes accessed 2026-06-27, Claude Code v2.1.191/v2.1.187 details, npm CLI v10, GitHub Copilot session-scope details, and Gemini main-branch docs.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Avoid implying stable behavior across versions without local validation.

mainFindings.finding: Local coding-agent security controls exist, but defaults differ by product and mode.
mainFindings.support: OpenAI Codex documents OS-enforced sandbox modes and network-off workspace-write behavior; Anthropic documents Claude Code read-only default permissions plus Bash sandbox behavior; Gemini CLI documents opt-in sandbox backends.

mainFindings.finding: Network controls should be treated as allowlist/risk-reduction mechanisms, not complete boundaries.
mainFindings.support: Codex keeps network off by default in workspace-write unless enabled; Claude sandbox starts with no domains pre-allowed and supports managed domain policy; GitHub Copilot cloud firewall is default but GitHub documents limitations and bypass/coverage caveats.

mainFindings.finding: Secrets and CI credentials require least privilege, short-lived credentials where practical, and protected access paths for untrusted PR/MR code.
mainFindings.support: GitHub Actions docs support least-privilege GITHUB_TOKEN permissions, masking/auditing, OIDC short-lived cloud credentials, log deletion and secret rotation after exposure; GitLab docs support fork/MR secret isolation and warn about parent-project pipeline exposure.

mainFindings.finding: Prompt injection is a first-class coding-agent risk because agents read untrusted repo, issue, PR, web, and log content.
mainFindings.support: OWASP LLM01 and NIST AI 600-1 identify direct/indirect prompt injection and recommend layered mitigations, including segregation, least privilege, validation, adversarial testing, and human approval for high-risk actions.

mainFindings.finding: Package-manager installs and builds are high-risk agent actions because common workflows can execute arbitrary package code.
mainFindings.support: npm docs list lifecycle scripts for install/ci and state scripts are passed to sh; pip secure-installs docs state default pip lacks remote-tampering protection and involves running arbitrary code from distributions, with hash/binary-only mitigations.

mainFindings.finding: A small-team feasible baseline is supportable from primary docs, but exact implementation varies by platform and agent.
mainFindings.support: GitHub and Anthropic docs support read-only/per-job permissions, required reviewers for protected environments, workflow review patterns, audited logs, and managed/project agent settings.

recommendations.recommendation: Set local agents to the narrowest workable mode: read-only for exploration, workspace-write for routine edits, and avoid full-access/bypass modes except in disposable environments with explicit human approval.
recommendations.support: OpenAI Codex, Claude Code, and Gemini CLI docs document sandbox/permission modes; Anthropic and GitHub caveats show sandboxes are not complete boundaries.

recommendations.recommendation: Default network to off or domain-allowlisted for agent sessions; require review before enabling broad internet access, especially when secrets or untrusted content are present.
recommendations.support: Codex network-off default and network_proxy policy, Claude domain approvals/managed settings, and GitHub Copilot firewall limitations support this as risk reduction rather than absolute isolation.

recommendations.recommendation: Harden CI for agent-authored or untrusted code: least-privilege job tokens, protected secrets/environments, no secrets for untrusted fork code unless explicitly reviewed, and short-lived/OIDC cloud credentials where supported.
recommendations.support: GitHub Actions and GitLab docs directly support least privilege, OIDC, fork/MR restrictions, and warnings about parent-context pipelines exposing secrets.

recommendations.recommendation: Class package installation, build, test, deploy, workflow edits, and dependency-policy changes as high-risk actions requiring either sandboxed ephemeral execution, script suppression/hash verification where practical, or human approval.
recommendations.support: npm and pip primary docs support arbitrary-code risk and mitigations; OWASP supports human approval for high-risk LLM actions.

recommendations.recommendation: Keep auditable agent and CI traces, but treat logs/transcripts as sensitive: mask secrets, review access, and delete exposed workflow logs while rotating leaked credentials if exposure occurs.
recommendations.support: GitHub Copilot session logs/commit links and GitHub Actions hardening docs support traceability, masking/auditing, deletion of exposed logs, and secret rotation.

recommendations.recommendation: Document a lightweight agent incident response playbook, but label it as adapted from general IR plus vendor-specific secret-exposure steps rather than a fully vendor-published agent IR standard.
recommendations.support: GitHub provides concrete exposed-secret/log steps; NIST/CISA are general IR backbones, not agent-specific playbooks.

actionPlan.step: Inventory which agent modes, CI runners, package managers, and secret paths are actually used by the team.
actionPlan.why: Controls differ by product and mode; inventory prevents applying Codex, Claude, Gemini, GitHub, or GitLab guidance to the wrong surface.

actionPlan.step: Apply default-deny local settings: restricted filesystem writes, network off/allowlisted, and no bypass/full-access mode in normal repositories.
actionPlan.why: Matches verified provider sandbox/network documentation and residual-risk caveats.

actionPlan.step: Update CI workflow defaults: read-only or least-privilege tokens, per-job permission escalation, protected environments/secrets, CODEOWNERS or equivalent review for workflow changes.
actionPlan.why: Supported by GitHub hardening docs and small-team baseline synthesis.

actionPlan.step: Create a high-risk-command policy covering package installs, build scripts, tests that run untrusted code, deploys, curl/wget/web fetches, and credential access.
actionPlan.why: Supported by Claude permission prompts, npm/pip arbitrary-code risks, and OWASP high-risk approval guidance.

actionPlan.step: Configure dependency safety defaults per ecosystem, such as frozen npm lockfile installs and script suppression where scripts are unnecessary, and pip hash/binary-only workflows where compatible.
actionPlan.why: npm/pip docs support behavior; parent must validate project compatibility before enforcing globally.

actionPlan.step: Define log handling: what to retain, who can view it, how secrets are masked, and how to delete logs/rotate credentials after exposure.
actionPlan.why: Traceability is useful, but logs can become a secret-exposure path.

actionPlan.step: Run a tabletop exercise for one prompt-injection scenario, one malicious dependency scenario, and one leaked-secret scenario.
actionPlan.why: Verifies whether the adapted IR and approval controls work in practice.

caveatedFindings.finding: Agent-specific incident response is only partially covered by primary sources.
caveatedFindings.caveat: GitHub gives concrete workflow-log deletion and secret-rotation steps, but broader agent incident procedures are adapted from NIST/CISA general IR guidance.
Some provider docs are live pages or main-branch repository docs; Gemini CLI behavior in particular should be validated against the installed release before enforcing exact settings.

caveatedFindings.finding: CI egress isolation is platform- and runner-specific.
caveatedFindings.caveat: Verified network-control recommendations are strong for named agent products, but GitHub/GitLab runner egress details should be verified against the team’s runner type before making strict allowlist claims.

unverifiedButRelevant.claim: Codex warns DNS rebinding protections are best-effort and danger-full-access disables sandbox/approvals.
unverifiedButRelevant.whyItMatters: Useful residual-risk language if parent discusses Codex hardening.

unverifiedButRelevant.claim: Anthropic says sandboxing is not a complete isolation boundary and lists TLS inspection, Unix socket, nested Linux, and Apple Events limitations.
OWASP says foolproof prompt-injection prevention is unclear and RAG/fine-tuning do not fully mitigate it.
GitHub enterprise audit log retention/search/export/stream details may provide concrete governance settings.
unverifiedButRelevant.evidenceStatus: preserved unverified lead; do not use exact retention numbers without verification

unverifiedButRelevant.claim: NIST SP 800-61 and CISA playbooks can structure agent incident response.
OpenSSF Scorecard says bot/AI reviews do not count as code review.
unverifiedButRelevant.whyItMatters: Could strengthen human-review policy for dependency/workflow changes.

unverifiedButRelevant.claim: GitLab recommends runner network segmentation, metadata-endpoint filtering, and runner-to-runner restrictions.
unverifiedButRelevant.whyItMatters: Could refine GitLab CI egress/isolation guidance.

unverifiedButRelevant.claim: GitHub-hosted runners are fresh VMs for most runner types, while self-hosted runners can be persistently compromised by untrusted code.
unverifiedButRelevant.whyItMatters: Could affect runner selection and trust assumptions.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Decide which agents and exact versions are in scope before converting this into policy.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: Defaults and sandbox mechanisms differ across Codex, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Copilot cloud agent, and CI platforms; some evidence is live-doc/main-branch scoped.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Pin policy language to named products and locally validated versions/settings.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Decide the team’s acceptable friction level for approvals.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: Human gates reduce risk for secrets, deploys, dependencies, and broad network/tool use, but too many prompts can push users toward bypass/full-access modes.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Use default approvals for high-impact actions only, and document an exception process for disposable sandboxes.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Decide whether dependency installs may run in normal developer worktrees or only in isolated ephemeral environments.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: npm and pip docs support arbitrary-code execution risk; strict settings may break legitimate project builds.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Start with frozen/hash-verified installs and script suppression where compatible; require approval or isolation when lifecycle/source-build code must run.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Decide what logs/transcripts are retained and who can access them.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: Traceability supports review and incident response, but logs can contain secrets or sensitive code/context.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Define retention/access locally; avoid citing exact enterprise retention numbers until verified for the account plan/platform.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Decide whether the parent needs broader IDE-agent coverage, such as Cursor or other assistants.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: Normalized verification coverage rests on Codex, Claude, Gemini, GitHub/GitLab, npm/pip, OWASP, and NIST; optional IDE-agent docs were not selected as verified candidates.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: If policy must cover a specific omitted IDE agent, run a follow-up product-specific verification.

researchScopeCoverage.localAgentSandboxing: covered for Codex, Claude Code, and Gemini CLI; Copilot cloud firewall covered separately
researchScopeCoverage.ciSecurity: covered for GitHub Actions and GitLab MR/fork variable exposure; runner egress detail remains platform-specific
researchScopeCoverage.secretsCredentials: covered with GitHub/GitLab primary docs and OIDC guidance
researchScopeCoverage.promptInjection: covered with OWASP and NIST guidance
researchScopeCoverage.dependencyBuildRisk: covered with npm and pip primary docs
researchScopeCoverage.auditGovernance: covered for Copilot/session and GitHub Actions concepts; exact enterprise retention numbers preserved but not verified
researchScopeCoverage.incidentResponse: partial: vendor-specific secret exposure steps plus general NIST/CISA adaptation

remainingGaps.gap: Agent-specific incident-response playbooks were not found as primary-source standards.
remainingGaps.whyItMatters: A parent-facing IR checklist should be labeled as adapted procedure, not vendor-authoritative agent IR.
remainingGaps.whatWouldChangeIfResolved: A verified agent-specific playbook could make incident steps more prescriptive and less caveated.

remainingGaps.gap: Some provider behavior is documented in live pages or main-branch docs rather than stable release-pinned artifacts.
remainingGaps.whyItMatters: Exact defaults/settings may change; policy should avoid over-precise version claims without local validation.
remainingGaps.whatWouldChangeIfResolved: Pinned release docs could support stricter product-specific configuration requirements.

remainingGaps.gap: CI egress controls vary by GitHub/GitLab runner type and configuration.
remainingGaps.whyItMatters: Network allowlisting recommendations may require different implementation for hosted versus self-hosted runners.
remainingGaps.whatWouldChangeIfResolved: Runner-specific verification could produce concrete egress architecture guidance.

remainingGaps.gap: Optional Cursor/other IDE-agent coverage was not verified.
remainingGaps.whyItMatters: If the parent intends a vendor-neutral policy covering every coding assistant used by a team, omitted products may have different defaults and bypass paths.
remainingGaps.whatWouldChangeIfResolved: Additional product-specific docs could add or alter local-agent control recommendations.

claims.support: Codex docs support OS sandboxing, default no network, workspace-limited writes, and read-only/workspace-write/danger-full-access modes.
claims.caveat: Startup behavior may recommend read-only in some folders.

claims.support: Codex network is off by default in workspace-write; experimental network_proxy can constrain enabled outbound traffic.
Claude Code read-only default and prompts for edits, tests, Bash, and network-fetching commands unless allowed.
Claude sandbox platform support and default read/write boundaries are directly documented.
claims.caveat: Broad default reads may include credential files unless denied.

claims.support: No domains pre-allowed; v2.1.191 host approval is session-scoped; managed settings can block non-allowed domains.
Gemini CLI sandbox enablement flags/settings and backends are documented.
claims.caveat: Main-branch evidence may vary by released version.

claims.support: Copilot cloud firewall defaults, allowlists, and limitations/bypass potential are documented.
GitHub supports least-privilege tokens, masking/auditing, OIDC, log deletion, and secret rotation.
claims.caveat: Read-only default refers specifically to repository contents access.

claims.support: GitLab fork MR variable isolation and parent-project exposure risk are documented.
OWASP recommends segregation, least privilege, filtering/validation, adversarial testing, and human approval.
claims.caveat: OWASP frames these as mitigations, not foolproof prevention.

claims.support: NIST identifies direct/indirect prompt injection and associated data theft/malicious-code risks.
claims.caveat: Wording uses proprietary data and remote malicious-code execution.

claims.support: npm install/ci lifecycle scripts and shell execution are documented.
claims.caveat: Exact shell behavior varies by platform/config.

claims.support: npm ci frozen lockfile behavior and ignore-scripts behavior are documented.
claims.caveat: Safer is an inferred risk-management recommendation.

claims.support: pip secure-installs docs support remote-tampering/arbitrary-code risk and require-hashes/only-binary mitigations.
claims.caveat: Does not eliminate all residual install risk.

claims.support: Copilot cloud agent logs, commit links, signed/Verified commits, sharing, archiving, and non-deletion are documented.
Primary docs support read-only/per-job permissions, secret/log auditing, required reviewers, CODEOWNERS workflow review, and managed/project settings.
claims.caveat: Small-team baseline is synthesized, not vendor terminology.
